case_description: [1] In January 2024, FastCharge AG, a battery technology company from Germany known for its fast-charging lithium solid-state cells, [2] publicly announced that it was unable to secure the necessary funds to move forward with its next-stage expansion plans. [3] The firm stated that a strained cash flow and tightening private capital markets were the key reasons for its stalled investment drive. [4] Two major VC companies that held talks with FastCharge from October to December 2023 announced that they stopped talks because, [5] after critical analysis of the company data and technology, [6] they considered further investment too risky. [7] Overall, FastCharge AG was seen as a company with two contrasting reputations. [8] Some people saw high potential in it as a critical player in Europe's energy transition and electric vehicle value chain. [9] Others assumed that the company was not able to compete with Asian producers and could not keep up with its technological promises. [10] Aware of these doubts but holding the view that FastCharge was the leading German battery company, [11] the German Federal Government, particularly the Ministry for Economic Affairs and Climate Action, expressed strong interest in supporting FastCharge AG. [12] Due to Germany’s tight fiscal position and the binding parameters of the national budget (Haushaltsgesetz), [13] no immediate funds could be made available. [15] The 2025 federal budget had already been finalized, [16] with no room left for new industrial support measures. [17] Faced with these constraints, the Bundestag adopted a resolution in March 2024 committing to free up fiscal space in the 2026 budget. [18] The resolution stated that, starting in Q1 2026, the government would inject a substantial equity investment into FastCharge AG, [19] contingent on the company remaining operational and meeting due diligence requirements. [20] Although no funds were transferred in 2024 or 2025, [21] the Bundestag’s decision had significant effects. [21] Immediately following the announcement, market analysts reassessed FastCharge AG’s risk profile. [22] The promise of future public investment led to an upgrade in the company’s credit rating and valuation. [23] By late 2024, FastCharge AG had secured two major contracts with European car manufacturers, [24] explicitly referencing the expected public capital injection in their partnership rationale. [25]. FastCharge’s CEO publicly credited the Bundestag’s forward-looking commitment as the decisive factor in regaining momentum. [26] As of today, no capital injection has taken place.
type_of_measure: Firm and Concrete Commitment to Invest
